key_insights.insight: Our brains have a negativity bias, making us prone to focusing on potential threats over positive aspects.
key_insights.quote: Now, your brain naturally goes toward anxiety because of something called the negativity bias. And I always think of it as 15 puppies and a cobra. If I gave you a box and it had 15 puppies and a cobra in it, what would catch your attention? The snake.

key_insights.insight: Sensory imagination, handled by the right hemisphere of the brain, can effectively reduce anxiety by shifting focus from verbal horror stories.
key_insights.quote: Because I asked you to use sensory imagination, and that's handled by the right hemisphere. It's not in the left. So instead of verbal imagination, which can create horror stories, you are in a sensory experience.

key_insights.insight: Being in a relaxed state allows you to approach anxious individuals with curiosity and empathy, becoming a calming presence.
key_insights.quote: So what you just did was move your nervous system into a state where you can be a field of peace for someone else who's anxious.

key_insights.insight: Engaging in activities that force your brain to create new neural pathways, like mirror writing, can promote deep learning and creativity.
key_insights.quote: You are fundamentally changing your brain, teaching it a skill it has never had. And this is what children are going through when they learn to write for the first time.

key_insights.insight: Calming anxiety involves treating it like a frightened animal, approaching it with gentleness and understanding rather than trying to fight it.
key_insights.quote: Because they think it's a broken machine, but it's not a broken machine, it's a frightened animal. And if you came in and I said to you, okay, I want to end you, I want to bring you down, I'm going to fight you till you're gone, would you be less afraid or more afraid?

key_insights.insight: Expressive writing, even without rereading or sharing, can significantly reduce anxiety and improve overall well-being by allowing frightened parts of ourselves to be heard.
key_insights.quote: So he, for his whole career, just did these writing exercises where he would have people just express themselves, not to show anyone, not even to reread, just to express. The parts of us that are frightened need to be heard.

key_insights.insight: Artistic expression can serve as a 'toggle switch' to turn off anxiety and engage creativity by distracting the grasping, fear-obsessed left-brain structures.
key_insights.quote: And there does seem to be this toggle effect that anxiety and creativity just can't work at the same time. So the moment you begin to create...all of a sudden, the toggle switches off in anxiety and on in creativity.

actionable_advice.advice: Use sensory imagination (e.g., vividly imagine eating an orange) to shift focus away from anxious thoughts.
actionable_advice.how_to: Close your eyes and deeply engage all senses - sight, smell, taste, touch, and sound - while imagining a relaxing sensory experience.

actionable_advice.advice: When feeling anxious about a situation with someone, observe your own anxiety and give it space.
actionable_advice.how_to: Notice where the anxiety manifests in your body, allow it to be there, and reassure the anxious part of yourself that you're present and will not try to stifle or make it go away.

actionable_advice.advice: Engage in making art to switch off anxiety and turn on creativity.
actionable_advice.how_to: Find an art activity (painting, spray-painting, making bead bracelets, etc) and lose yourself in the process without worrying about the outcome.

actionable_advice.advice: Write about things that are upsetting you, and allow yourself to fully express your feelings.
actionable_advice.how_to: Set a timer for 15 minutes and write continuously about something upsetting, without worrying about grammar or structure. Don't reread or share what you've written.

memorable_stories: The story of Chris Voss, an FBI hostage negotiator, using gentleness and empathy to deal with violent terrorists.
A story of giving four and five-year-olds a creativity test where 98% of them where creative geniuses.
The story of Truth and Reconciliation councils in South Africa.
Groups of clients being taken to the forest and they have to make fire with sticks and rocks without talking.
contrarian_claims: Attacking anxiety or trying to force it to calm down only makes it worse; the best approach is to treat it with gentleness and understanding.
Our culture pushes the human spirit to the fringes.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Most Replayed Moment: Instantly Calm Your Anxiety -  Dr. Martha Beck
